rulings.Case Title: JOSEPH D. STEWART VS. AIRBNB INC. ET AL
rulings.Rulings: Matter on the Law and Motion calendar for Monday, October 7, 2024, Line 12. PLAINTIFF JOSEPH STEWART's PETITION TO VACATE ARBITRATION. Plaintiff Joseph Stewart's motion to vacate arbitrators award is denied. Stewart, a California attorney in pro per, sued Airbnb in 2021 for cancelling his license and causing him to lose bookings. This court compelled arbitration, and the arbitrator ruled for Airbnb. The arbitrator found that Airbnb was entitled to terminate Stewart's listings without compensation based on Stewart's material breach of the Airbnb terms of service. Stewart now moves to vacate under CCP 1286.2 (a)(1), (3) and (4). First, nothing in the record evinces an award was procured by corruption, fraud, or other undue means. (CCP 1286.2 (a)(1).) Second, Stewart's rights were not substantially prejudiced by any misconduct from the arbitrator. (CCP 1286.2 (a)(4).) Stewart argues the arbitrator prejudiced him by denying discovery motions on written discovery requests and international witness subpoenas. The arbitrator did not engage in misconduct but merely exercised his authority to deny improper, disproportionate discovery requests. In any event, Stewart has not shown these discovery rulings materially prejudiced him in presenting this relatively straightforward case. Third, Stewart has not shown the arbitrator exceeded his powers by only allowing him to increase his demand to $200,000, instead of the full $1,500,000 requested. (CCP 1286.2 (a)(3).) The arbitrator issued a reasoned "Order re Increase of Claim, Regular Track, Further Scheduling," that interpreted the applicable AAA rules on amendments. (Stewart decl. exh. 8.) To extent the ruling was erroneous, this error of law is not reviewable here. Stewart also contends the arbitrator failed to move the case to "Regular Track Procedures," but the same order shows the arbitrator did so. (Id. para. 4.) Finally, Stewart complains the parties did not mediate but does not explain how this would be grounds for vacatur. In any event, the rule Stewart cites allows parties to opt out of mediation. (Stewart decl. exh. 17, p. 171.) As Steward has not shown grounds for vacating the award, the petition is denied. For the 9:30 a.m. Law & Motion calendar, all attorneys and parties may appear in Department 302 remotely. Remote hearings will be conducted by videoconference using Zoom. To appear remotely at the hearing, go to the court's website at sfsuperiorcourt.org under "Online Services," navigate to "Tentative Rulings," and click on the appropriate link, or dial the corresponding phone number. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. The text of the email shall include the name and contact information, including email address, of the attorney or party who will appear at the hearing. Counsel for the prevailing party is required to prepare a proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must email it to contestdept302tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the tentative ruling is not contested. The court no longer provides a court reporter in the Law & Motion Department. Parties may retain their own reporter, who may appear in the courtroom or remotely. A retained reporter must be a California certified court reporter (CSR), for only a CSR's transcript may be used in California courts. If a CSR is being retained, include in your email all of the following: their name, CSR and telephone numbers, and their individual work email address. =(302/RBU)

rulings.Case Title: ARASH KHALATBARI VS. EPIC REAL ESTATE AND MANAGMENT, INC. ET AL
rulings.Rulings: Matter on the Law and Motion calendar for Monday, October 7, 2024, Line 4. DEFENDANT EPIC REAL ESTATE & ASSO. INC's MOTION FOR SUMMARY ADJUDICATION. Defendant's motion for summary adjudication is denied. There is a triable issue of material fact whether plaintiff signed the cited independent contractor agreement. "The declarations in support of a motion for summary judgment [or adjudication] should be strictly construed, while the opposing declarations should be liberally construed." (Fernandez v. Alexander (2019) 31 Cal.App.5th 770, 779.) The provisions of the Unemployment Insurance Code incorporated in Business and Professions Code section 10032(b) provide that a real estate agent is an independent contractor if three conditions are satisfied: (1) the agent is licensed; (2) the agent is paid through commissions; and (3) the agent has signed an independent contractor agreement. (See Whitlach v. Premier Valley (2022) 86 Cal. App. 5th 673, 690.) In this case, defendant asserts that plaintiff signed an independent contractor agreement on February 25, 2019. (UMF 9.) Plaintiff, however, contests that he ever signed that agreement and the conflicting evidence creates a triable issue of material fact that the trier of fact must resolve. (Azari Decl., par. 18, Ex. 3; Khalatbari Decl., pars. 9-11.) For the 9:30 a.m. Law & Motion calendar, all attorneys and parties may appear in Department 302 remotely. Remote hearings will be conducted by videoconference using Zoom. To appear remotely at the hearing, go to the court's website at sfsuperiorcourt.org under "Online Services," navigate to "Tentative Rulings," and click on the appropriate link, or dial the corresponding phone number. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. The text of the email shall include the name and contact information, including email address, of the attorney or party who will appear at the hearing. Counsel for the prevailing party is required to prepare a proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must email it to contestdept302tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the tentative ruling is not contested. The court no longer provides a court reporter in the Law & Motion Department. Parties may retain their own reporter, who may appear in the courtroom or remotely. A retained reporter must be a California certified court reporter (CSR), for only a CSR's transcript may be used in California courts. If a CSR is being retained, include in your email all of the following: their name, CSR and telephone numbers, and their individual work email address. =(302/RBU)

rulings.Case Title: SUSAN MAC CORMAC TAYLOR ET AL VS. JERRY DRATLER ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: MOTION FOR SUMMARY JUDGMENT On Plaintiffs Count For Invasion Of Privacy
rulings.Rulings: Matter on the Law and Motion calendar for Monday, October 7, 2024, Line 5. DEFENDANT JERRY DRATLER's MOTION FOR SUMMARY JUDGMENT On Plaintiffs Count For Invasion Of Privacy. Defendant's motion is denied. Defendant failed to maintain its burden of production and dispose of plaintiff's entire cause of action for invasion of privacy. (CCP 437c(f)(1).) Defendant's motion merely addresses plaintiffs' contention that defendant's four photographs invaded plaintiffs' privacy. The motion does not address plaintiffs' request for injunctive relief and the pleadings determine materiality. (Conroy v. Regents of University of California (2009) 45 Cal.4th 1244, 1250.) In any event, there are triable issues of material fact. At UMF 24, defendant states that his "sole intent" was to take photographs to include in his Discretionary Review Application. (Dratler Decl., par. 11.) At deposition, however, defendant admitted that he "really wasn't' sure if and when and how I was going to use [the photographs]." (Quadra Decl., Ex. E [Dratler Depo., 201:19-20].) Defendant also would not commit to refraining from taking photographs in the future even though the Discretionary Review process was over. (Id. a 226:1-24.) This evidence creates a triable issue of fact regarding defendant's intent. (Nazir v. United Airlines, Inc. (2009) 178 Cal.App.4th 243, 252 [inclusion of a fact in a separate statement concedes its materiality]; Salazar v. Thomas (2015) 236 Cal.App.4th 467, 475 fn. 6 ["[T]he separate statement effectively concedes the materiality of whatever facts are included. Thus, if a triable issue is raised as to any of the facts in [such a] separate statement, the motion must be denied!" (internal quotations omitted)].) For the 9:30 a.m. Law & Motion calendar, all attorneys and parties may appear in Department 302 remotely. Remote hearings will be conducted by videoconference using Zoom. To appear remotely at the hearing, go to the court's website at sfsuperiorcourt.org under "Online Services," navigate to "Tentative Rulings," and click on the appropriate link, or dial the corresponding phone number. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. The text of the email shall include the name and contact information, including email address, of the attorney or party who will appear at the hearing. Counsel for the prevailing party is required to prepare a proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must email it to contestdept302tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the tentative ruling is not contested. The court no longer provides a court reporter in the Law & Motion Department. Parties may retain their own reporter, who may appear in the courtroom or remotely. A retained reporter must be a California certified court reporter (CSR), for only a CSR's transcript may be used in California courts. If a CSR is being retained, include in your email all of the following: their name, CSR and telephone numbers, and their individual work email address. =(302/RBU)

rulings.Case Title: ELIZABETH HSU VS. THE REGENTS OF THE UNIVERSITY OF CALIFORNIA ET AL
rulings.Rulings: Matter on the Law and Motion calendar for Monday, October 7, 2024, Line 13. DEFENDANT THE REGENTS OF THE UNIVERSITY OF CALIFORNIA's DEMURRER to 1ST Amended COMPLAINT. Defendant's demurrer to Elizabeth Hsu's four-page first amended complaint (FAC) is sustained with leave to amend. The FAC is too skimpy even by California's lenient standards. It first seeks to plead "failure to accommodate disability" based on hand and hip surgeries in 2019 and 2022. (FAC 2:11-13.) But a physical condition must "limit[] a major life activity" to be a disability, and the FAC does not suggest the surgeries were other than transitory. (Govt. Code 12926(m)(B); CACI 2541(3).) The FAC next seeks to plead age and "racial or national origin" discrimination on little more than Hsu's 40+ status and "Asian ancestry." (Id. at 3:7, 24.) Finally, the FAC's refusal-to-rehire "retaliation" theory merits more than the one sentence accorded it. (Id. at 4:15-17.) For the 9:30 a.m. Law & Motion calendar, all attorneys and parties may appear in Department 302 remotely. Remote hearings will be conducted by videoconference using Zoom.
